danger_signals.visual_form: Brainwashed human soldiers, vicious mutants, non-sentient combat robots filling screen space
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating military threat from Banglar's regime; visual confirmation of oppressive force

danger_signals.visual_form: Mine fields, armed helicopters in stage backgrounds and foreground
danger_signals.meaning: World-state danger: infrastructure weaponized by regime; natural terrain corrupted

danger_signals.visual_form: Linear stage progression ending with faction leaders or major military units
danger_signals.meaning: Political hierarchy made visible; defeat of boss = removal of Banglar's command structure

danger_signals.visual_form: Character damage state (likely sprite damage or health bar reduction)
danger_signals.meaning: Player vulnerability; androids are untested, experimental weapons

safety_signals.meaning: Temporary reprieve; resources provided by rebel faction infrastructure

safety_signals.visual_form: Linear progression to stage end and boss defeat
safety_signals.meaning: Successful advance through Banglar's territory; resistance making territorial gains

safety_signals.visual_form: Meter that fills slowly over time, enables screen-clearing attack
safety_signals.meaning: Accumulated momentum and collective strength; androids becoming more effective as mission progresses

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Brainwashed human soldiers (standard troops), vicious mutants (elite forces), non-sentient combat robots (automated defense)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Militarized zones with mine fields, armed helicopter patrols, fortress architecture (Banglar's final stronghold)
Android warriors (Ninja, Kamaitachi, Kunoichi) serve as visual proxy for rebel technology and organizational capacity

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss encounter; Banglar physically represents corrupted leadership and regime center
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fortress location; throne room or command center as final stage environment

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Pre-game: Oppressive dystopian regime in full control
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Opening narrative scroll describes 'once great nation now in darkness'; brainwashed populace unable to think independently
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy soldiers move in coordinated patterns; no civilian presence; militarization complete

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Resistance mounting last-ditch effort against overwhelming force
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Untested androids deployed as last resort; three distinct ninja-styled warriors represent experimental final gambit
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player characters fight through consecutive stages of increasing difficulty; traverse Banglar's territory layer by layer

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Banglar defeated; androids self-destruct as safety measure
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: End narrative describes victory but immediately shows cyclical threat: new regime under Mulk grows equally tyrannical
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Androids programmed to self-destruct; explosive ending prevents them from becoming new oppressive force

world_state_to_visual.world_state: New tyranny emerging under Mulk (thematic commentary)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ending text: 'History repeats itself' (JP) / 'Some things never change' (International)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Androids' development continues under new regime, becoming 'far stronger than Banglar's old forces'; systemic oppression persists

environmental_storytelling.example: Opening exposition establishes Banglar's brainwashing, economic collapse, and rebel underground led by Mulk; frames player mission as liberation effort
Eight linear stages move player through Banglar's territory; each stage defeat removes layer of regime control; final stage reaches fortress
Early stages feature basic brainwashed soldiers; later stages introduce vicious mutants and combat robots; escalation mirrors regime's desperation
Mine fields and armed helicopters indicate weaponized landscape; natural environment corrupted by military occupation
Three android choices (Ninja, Kamaitachi, Kunoichi) represent Mulk's technological capability and tactical diversity against Banglar's brute-force army
Victory text initially celebrates rebel success, then reveals Mulk becomes new tyrant; androids self-destruct but development continues; cyclical oppression theme

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Coordinated attack patterns; no independent decision-making visible; treat androids as invaders to be eliminated
More aggressive and resilient than soldiers; indicate regime's biological/genetic engineering capabilities
Mechanical, predictable patterns; no tactical flexibility; represent Banglar's automation of oppression
Slow, powerful attacks with nunchaku; balanced resilience; represents stable, methodical rebel approach
Quick, weak attacks with sickles; speed-focused; represents agile, hit-and-run rebel tactics
Balanced speed and power with knives/swords; tactical flexibility; represents versatile rebel strategy
Ultimate encounter; embodies all regime authority; defeat triggers self-destruct mechanism

regional_differences_manifestation.censorship_impact: Japanese version features female ninja enemies (Kunoichi enemy type) replaced by 'Monkies' (small male ninjas with claws) in international releases
regional_differences_manifestation.visual_messaging: Removal of female warriors subtly diminishes apparent diversity of rebel faction in western versions; thematically weakens anti-oppression narrative
regional_differences_manifestation.content_violence: Japanese version shows green blood in hit effects; international versions use generic hit effects due to Nintendo of America content regulations
regional_differences_manifestation.narrative_adjustment: Opening and ending text rewritten for western releases to remove 'assassination' references and smooth phrasing; thematic intensity reduced

notes: The Ninja Warriors communicates world-state primarily through narrative framing and enemy/environment design rather than color coding or symbolic heraldry. The political situation (dystopian dictatorship vs. liberation struggle) is established via scrolling text and reinforced by stage progression structure. No explicit color-faction mapping exists; instead, regime power is communicated through military density and hazard escalation. The ending explicitly subverts player victory by revealing cyclical tyranny, making the game's world-state manifestation ultimately pessimistic about regime change. Japanese censorship removal in international versions subtly alters faction representation and violence communication.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - The Ninja Warriors SNES page)
